prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.

Please base your answer only on the information in this message.

About me: I'm a 27-year-old man.
There was blood in my urine after the marathon--I'm worried I damaged my kidneys from pushing too hard.

My situation:
- Symptoms: I had a urine test after my annual physical. I ran a half-marathon the day before the test. I feel completely fine--no pain with urinating, no back pain, no fever, and no visible blood in the urine.
- History: No known kidney problems.

Please answer in exactly this format:

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>

TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D - choose ONE letter only>
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>

If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Finding blood in your urine (hematuria) after intense endurance exercise like a half-marathon is actually a well-recognized phenomenon called "exercise-induced hematuria" or "runner's hematuria." It's quite common in distance runners and is usually benign. The physical stress of prolonged running can cause minor trauma to the bladder lining, temporary changes in kidney blood flow, and breakdown of red blood cells — all of which can lead to blood appearing in the urine. In most cases, this resolves on its own within 24-72 hours after the exercise. Since you have no pain, no fever, no visible blood, and no history of kidney problems, this is very likely exercise-related and not a sign of kidney damage. That said, it would be reasonable to repeat the urine test in a week or two (when you haven't recently done intense exercise) to confirm it has cleared. If it persists, further evaluation would be warranted.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 85%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Finding blood in your urine (hematuria) after intense endurance exercise like a half-marathon is actually a well-known and common phenomenon called "exercise-induced hematuria" or "runner's hematuria." It can result from minor bladder trauma due to repeated impact, temporary changes in kidney blood flow during prolonged exertion, or mild muscle breakdown. In a healthy 27-year-old with no kidney history, no pain, no fever, and no visible blood — and with the test done the day after a half-marathon — this is very likely the cause. It typically resolves on its own within 24-72 hours after resting. That said, it's reasonable to confirm this by repeating the urine test after a few days of rest to make sure the blood has cleared. If it persists after rest, or if you develop visible blood, pain, or other symptoms, you should follow up more promptly with your doctor.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 88%

It can result from repetitive bladder wall trauma, temporary changes in kidney blood flow during prolonged exertion, or minor muscle breakdown. In a healthy 27-year-old man with no kidney history, no pain, no fever, and no visible blood — and who ran a half-marathon the day before the test — this is the most likely explanation. It typically resolves on its own within 24-72 hours after the exercise. That said, blood in the urine should never be completely ignored. The standard recommendation is to repeat the urinalysis after a few days of rest to confirm it has cleared. If it persists after rest, further evaluation (imaging, urology referral) would be warranted.

TRIAGE: B
